evidence.page_title: Liverpool set for busiest weekend of the year with the Champions ...
evidence.title: All you need to know as Liverpool set for busiest weekend ever
evidence.description: Hundreds of thousands of people are expected to flood the city centre on Saturday and Sunday
evidence.text:
Liverpool set for busiest weekend of the year with the Champions League final and River Festival
Hundreds of thousands of people are expected to flood the city centre on Saturday and Sunday
Liverpool is set to host its busiest weekend EVER with hundreds of thousands of people expected to flock to the city.
With Reds fans flocking to city centre venues to watch LFC take on Tottenham in the Champions League final and the River Festival taking place at the docks, Liverpool is expected to be extremely busy.
Advice has been given out to those planning on heading to Liverpool for any one of the events taking place here.
Merseyside Police, Liverpool City Council, Culture Liverpool, Merseytravel, British Transport Police and Liverpool John Lennon Airport will be working together this weekend to ensure that visitors to the city have a fantastic time, in a safe environment.
Many people are also expected to attend the Bordeaux Wine Festival throughout Saturday and Sunday bringing even more tourism to the city.
Throughout the weekend there will also be extra police officers in the city centre to ensure people can go about their business safely and enjoy all that the night time economy of Liverpool has to offer.
Merseyrail have also said that due to the large volumes of passengers expected to travel to and from Liverpool, that their services may be subject to change.
Top news stories
Here is everything you need to know about what is happening this weekend.
River Festival
The River Festival is returning to Liverpool for 2019 and there’s an exciting events programme planned.
Highlights for the free event include artwork on display such as Gaia, a giant illuminated earth replica, and the return of Bordeaux Wine Festival.
Deputy Mayor and cabinet member for culture, tourism and events, Councillor Wendy Simon, said: "River Festival Liverpool has become a much-loved staple of our events calendar and it attracts hundreds of thousands of people each year.
"We are incredibly proud of the creative programme which showcases some of the very best in art and culture from local and global talent through commissions, installations and performances.
"It’s set to be a busy weekend on our UNESCO World Heritage Waterfront as we celebrate our maritime heritage."
Liverpool fans create perfect banner for Joel Matip in Madrid for Champions League finalThe award-winning Bordeaux Wine Festival returns to Liverpool this weekend with an offering of world-class wine and food.
The event will run from May 31 to June 2, and tickets cost £21.50.
Tickets can be booked here.
Champions League final
If it is anything like last year, Liverpool will be turning red on Saturday in support of LFC in the Champions League final.
In May 2018, fans managed to shut down the city centre by singing, chanting and enjoying themselves in the streets and it is likely that Saturday could be even bigger.
Throughout the weekend there will be extra police officers in the city centre to ensure people can go about their business safely and enjoy all that the night time economy of Liverpool has to offer.
Superintendent Mark Wiggins, said: "We know that this weekend will certainly be a busy time for our region’s pubs, clubs and bars and we want people to come into our city and enjoy themselves.
"There will be a large number of people in the city centre throughout the weekend and we are expecting large numbers of people to come in to the city to the watch the Champions League Final at various venues throughout the city centre on Saturday night.
“We want people coming in to Liverpool city centre to enjoy themselves and our message to people coming into the city is – take some simple steps; keep your bags fastened and phones hidden, stay together and drink responsibly.
"If you’re coming in to the city on Saturday night to have a few drinks and watch the game make it a night to remember, not a night to forget."
Merseyrail users are also asked to plan the journeys in advance and leave plenty of time to get into town and home again and services will be busier than normal.
Victory Parade
In the hopeful event of Liverpool winning the Champions League, a victory parade has been planned for Sunday, June 2.
The team will parade the trophy along a route starting at 4pm at Allerton Maze, then heading through the city, along The Strand to Blundell Street.
Love Island 2019 - First look inside the revamped villa complete with luxury hideawayHuge crowds are expected to turn out to see their favourite players cheering on the back of the red Liverpool bus - so Liverpool City Council has issued advice to help make sure you get to enjoy the experience safely.
The Champions League parade would take place when Liverpool is already staging two major events – River Festival Liverpool and the Bordeaux Wine Festival - which are already expected to attract tens of thousands of people to the waterfront.
Therefore, The River Festival Liverpool event would start earlier than advertised on Sunday to accommodate a parade.
Parade route and map
The route starts at Allerton Maze (the junction where Allerton Road crosses over with Queens Drive) and will travel north bound on Queens Drive towards the Fiveways roundabout and Rocket flyover. From there it will journey along:
- Queens Drive
- Mill Bank
- West Derby Road
- Islington
- Leeds Street
- The Strand
- Route finishes at Blundell Street
Advice from Liverpool City Council
- Use the full length of the route. The open top bus won’t be stopping so take advantage of the full length of the route from Queens Drive all the way to The Strand.
- Decide which part of the route you want to wait at and arrive in plenty of time.
- Be patient – it is impossible to predict how long it will take the team bus to travel along the route.
- Due to the number of people coming in to and out of the city, there will be significant demands on public transport, with queuing likely.
Travelling on Merseyrail
Saturday 1 June
Train services will be strengthened where possible for passengers travelling to watch the match and support LFC.
There will be no timetable changes, however:
- Northern line passengers should use Moorfields station
- Wirral line passengers should use Liverpool Central station
The last trains departing the city centre after the match are expected to be extremely busy. Please check your train times, leaving plenty of time for your journey and possible queues at stations.
For passengers attending the match screening at Aintree racecourse, the last trains after the match depart Aintree for Liverpool at 22:54, 23:24 and 23:54.
Sunday 2 June
Due to the amount of planning involved, the parade needs to be organised in advance and transport arrangements put in place for fans to celebrate with the club, with train being the most effective way of getting into the city.
Tickets can be purchased from any Merseyrail station before travelling.
Frequent services will run throughout the day, strengthened to six cars wherever possible.
There will be some minor timetable changes, but the frequency of trains will remain the same as a normal Sunday.
Due to the size of crowds expected, some stations will be closed. These are:
- Lime Street low level
- James Street
- Green Lane
- Bromborough Rake
- Capenhurst
In addition:
- Northern line trains will only call at Moorfields in the city centre
- Wirral line trains will only call at Liverpool Central in the city centre
Full timetables can be found on the Merseyrail website HERE.

evidence.page_title: Plans for huge Liverpool Champions League victory parade along ...
evidence.title: Liverpool's potential Champions League victory parade revealed
evidence.description: Tens of thousands of people are expected to line the city's streets if the Reds return from Kiev victorious
evidence.text:
Revealed: Plans for huge Liverpool Champions League victory parade along city's historic waterfront if Reds win in Kiev
Tens of thousands of people are expected to line the city's streets if the Reds return from Kiev victorious
Plans are being put in place for a victory parade through Liverpool if the Reds are victorious in this month's Champions League final.
If Jurgen Klopp's team triumph in Kiev on Saturday May 26, Liverpool City Council will formally invite the club to hold a parade in the city on the following day - a date requested by bosses at Anfield.
Because of the major logistical challenges needed to arrange a parade of this scale, an announcement is being made ahead of the final, which will see the Reds take on Real Madrid.
The potential parade will take place when Liverpool is already staging two major events - Three Festivals Tall Ships Regatta and the Bordeaux Wine Festival - which alone are expected to attract tens of thousands of people to the waterfront.
If Liverpool are successful in Kiev, it will start from midday on May 27, last for a couple of hours and see the Liverpool FC team parade the Champions League trophy on an open top bus, travelling along Liverpool’s UNESCO World Heritage Waterfront.
The route starts at the Parliament Street/The Strand junction, and will journey along:
· The Strand
· Up Leeds Street
· Along Hunter Street
· Along Islington
· Along Erskine Street
· Along West Derby Road
· The parade will finish near the Liverpool Olympia
The Three Festivals Tall Ships Regatta event will take place later than advertised on Sunday to accommodate the parade if it takes place. The new start time of the maritime celebration will be advertised next week.
Mayor of Liverpool Joe Anderson said: “Regardless of whether you are a Red, Blue or not a football fan at all, an event like this has a hugely positive impact and will once again shine the international spotlight on the city.
READ MORE: Liverpool hotel room prices rocket to £1,400 for night of Champions League final“We are all tremendously proud of LFC’s achievement and you can already feel the buzz around the city ahead of the final.
“The club requested the parade takes place on the Sunday, and even though it’s already a busy day for the city, we want to make sure we are ready to give them the heroes’ welcome they deserve should they win.
“I understand there will be disappointment for those fans who are unable to get back from Kiev in time, but it was a similar situation in 2005 and we have to be guided by the club in terms of the date and time.}
“We are working closely with LFC, Merseyside Police and our travel partners to ensure that the event runs smoothly and safely for everyone to enjoy.
“We’re keeping everything crossed for the final and hope the team and the fans do Liverpool proud in Kiev as they have throughout the competition.”
The parade is expected to attract tens of thousands of people, so the city council is issuing key advice for fans:
Use the full length of the route. The open top bus won’t be stopping so take advantage of the full length of the route from the bottom Parliament Street, all the way to West Derby Road.
Decide which part of the route you want to wait at and arrive in plenty of time.
Be patient – it is impossible to predict how long it will take the team bus to travel along the route.
Due to the number of people coming in to and out of the city, there will be significant demands on public transport, with queuing likely.
Motorists are advised that the city centre is expected to be extremely busy, and with this in mind they should plan their journeys in and out of the city carefully.
Merseyside Police Superintendent Mark Wiggins said: “Should Liverpool win the cup then the parade will take place and we would expect a large number of people to gather along the route. We will be supporting Liverpool City Council, who are organising the event, to ensure the event goes ahead safely and disruption to regular road users and people living and visiting the city can be minimised as much as possible.
“Along with the fans, the public will see a large number of police officers who will be highly visible throughout the area of the city centre and the route of the parade. They’ll be providing a reassuring presence and making sure the day is not only one to remember, but also an enjoyable and safe event.
“I would ask people intending to come to this parade to ensure they given themselves enough time to arrive and decide how they are going to get to the city given how busy the public transport networks and the roads will be. I would also ask that people use the whole route to view the parade because you will see exactly the same thing wherever you stand along the route to see it.
“Anyone attending needs to be sensible and ensure their own safety and I would advise against putting yourself or others in danger by climbing anything to see the parade. Given the players will be on a bus there is no need to do this and you will be able to see adequately from the side of the road.
“We wish Liverpool every success and are proud of their achievements in representing this city!”
It is expected there will be significant disruption around the parade route and travel advice and information will be made available as soon as possible. However, key points include:
Consider walking and use public transport wherever possible.
Be prepared for some changes to services, particularly for bus due to diversions.
Allow time for and plan your journey. The transport network is expected to be very busy before and after the parade.
Check timetables - be prepared for some changes to services, particularly bus for which some diversions will be in place.
The Mersey Ferries are a good cross river option with regular services throughout the day.
Wayne Menzies Merseytravel’s Head of Rail and Chair of the Major Events Transport Board, said: “We have been working closely with transport operators, Liverpool City Council, Merseyside Police and other partners to ensure that we are doing all we can to help people get to the parade and home again safely, if it does go ahead.
“It’s really important that people plan their journeys and there will be plenty of advice and information available to keep them up to date on the travel picture.”
‘Live’ travel updates and alerts will be available from Merseytravel at www.merseytravel.gov.uk. Consider following @Merseytravel, #LFCparade and transport operators on Twitter for the latest information.

evidence.page_title: Liverpool's potential Champions League parade could be EXTENDED
evidence.title: Liverpool's potential Champions League parade route could be EXTENDED
evidence.description: Council event chiefs have spoken exclusively to the ECHO and revealed why the potential route was chosen
evidence.text:
Liverpool's potential Champions League parade could be EXTENDED - as event chiefs explain thinking behind it
Council event chiefs have spoken exclusively to the ECHO and revealed why the potential route was chosen
Discussions are ongoing which could result in Liverpool’s potential Champions League parade route being EXTENDED.
The council and club faced a backlash after details of a potential victory parade were revealed - with complaints over the timing and route choice.
Now the ECHO can reveal talks are ongoing which could see the route that a victorious team bus would potentially take being extended.
The choice to hold a potential parade just a day after the Kiev final on Sunday, May 27 was made by the club, with players set to join up with international squads the following day.
Under the plans, the bus carrying the team would start at the junction of Parliament Street and The Strand, travelling along the main road and up Leeds Street.
It would then journey along Hunter Street, Islington and Erskine Street, before heading on to West Derby Road and finishing near to the Liverpool Olympia.
In an exclusive interview with the ECHO, event planners at the city council have explained why a route along the Strand was taken and other notable areas like St George’s Plateau, Edge Lane and Anfield stadium were avoided.
It has also been revealed that talks are currently ongoing between all sides to see whether the route could become larger.
We spoke to city event managers Sue Lees and Andy McNichol, who explained the thinking behind the route plan and explained in detail why the areas listed above were left out.
Champions League final
Sue said the team was somewhat shocked by the level of criticism the proposals were met with by fans yesterday.
She said: “We have put a lot of time, effort and expertise in to come up with a route that takes in all the considerations.
“It is one of the biggest weekends that the city is going to see for a long time, so there are so many things to take into account.
“We were shocked at the reaction and I think 2005 is playing a big role in that. I think a lot of people don’t understand that 2005 was a unique route because The Strand was being dug up for roadworks, traditionally we have always gone down the Strand.
“You get the iconic buildings in the shots, it’s incredibly wide and has a huge viewing capacity - it also means the main transport hubs can get everyone in and out of the city and with these other events on it felt like the best route to take the bus down.”
Andy said it was essential to point out that the same route being proposed for May 27 was signed off in both 2014 and 2016 before it transpired that a parade would not take place.
Here are the team’s explanations of why popular spots have not been included.
St George’s Hall
Andy said: “It was considered, it isn’t just as making decisions, there are many different stake holders involved.
“We did the event in 2005 and there were a lot of concerns about what happened then, with one big concern about the impact on the transport hubs on the night in 2005.
“Effectively it closed down Lime Street Station and Queen Square bus station.
“If you close down the main bus and train stations in the city, you don’t have to be a transport expert to imagine the knock-on effect that will have.
“The other fact is that St George’s is not massive, it can’t accommodate the number of people who will want to come.
“As soon as you put St George’s down as a part of the route, it becomes a focal point and the area can’t cope.”
Edge Lane
Sue said: “We’ve already got two major events on ourselves and a UFC event at the arena, plus all the normal workings of the city.
“If we are going to put a parade down the main corridor of the city we will shut the city down, our basic rule in events planning is if you shut the Strand you need to keep Lime Street open and Edge Lane running and visa versa, we can’t do both as it would gridlock the city.”
Anfield
Sue said: “One of the main reasons is the roads around Anfield, they are so narrow. Anfield is in the middle of a residential area. The roads are incredibly tight.
“Anfield would become a focal point with thousands of people coming in and it isn’t equipped.
“It’s a completely different dynamic to when people are heading to the match and they have a system in place to get people quickly into the stadium.”
Andy added: “Another factor is the transport network around Anfield, it’s great on match days for 55,000 people - but there isn’t a big train or bus station to transport 200,000 people. We could have had a situation with hundreds of thousands of people and no way to get home.”
READ MORE: Joe Anderson responds to criticism over Liverpool Champions League parade routeRoute extended?
The events team confirmed that discussions are now ongoing which could see the route being extended, but said nothing had been decided at this stage.
Sue said: “We are looking at the route and seeing if there are any options, but nothing has been decided as of yet.
“It’s not as easy as saying yes let’s do it, there are road works taking place that have to be considered as well.
“There are discussions going on which could see the route extended.”
Mayor’s statement:
Speaking about the route choice, Mayor Joe Anderson released the following statement:
“My number one priority as Mayor of Liverpool is the safety of the people of this city.
“I understand some of the frustrations which have been expressed by fans, but am happy to accept that criticism, knowing that the route and plan which has been put in place is the safest and most manageable for an event such as this.
“I am proud of the huge amount of work which organisations and individuals from across the city have done to design an event which will allow the maximum number of people to take part in a safe and deliverable way.
READ MORE: Revealed: Plans for huge Liverpool Champions League victory parade along city's historic waterfront if Reds win in Kiev“The teams working on it will only have 12 hours’ notice of it taking place and can only estimate the number of people they will be dealing with. The added complexity of this is the 250,000 people expected to be in the city over 3 days for the Tall Ships and Bordeaux Wine Festival event.
“Some of the suggestions for locations for the route were carefully considered from the outset and decided against – by all agencies involved - for very real concerns about management and safety.
“The route is the most manageable while also providing the truly iconic backdrop of our Waterfront.”

evidence.page_title: Where to get the best view of Liverpool's Champions League parade
evidence.title: Where to get the best view of Liverpool's Champions League parade
evidence.description: Thousands of fans are expected to head out to catch a glimpse of the bus if the result goes Liverpool's way
evidence.text:
Where to get the best view of Liverpool FC's Champions League victory parade
Thousands of fans are expected to head out to catch a glimpse of the bus if the result goes Liverpool's way
Liverpool fans will be hoping the Reds can bring home the Champions League trophy for a sixth time when they travel to Madrid for the final against Tottenham Hotspur on June 1.
If the result goes Liverpool's way, a victory parade will go ahead on Sunday, June 2, with them team celebrating on top of a bus, which will travel through the city.
The bus route will start at 4pm at Allerton Maze (the junction where Allerton Road crosses over with Queens Drive) and will travel north bound on Queens Drive towards the Childwall Fiveways roundabout and Rocket Flyover.
From there it will journey along Mill Bank, West Derby Road, Islington, Leeds Street, The Strand and will finish at Blundell Street.
These Liverpool bars have already sold out for the Champions League finalTaking a look back to 2005, the victory parade that followed Liverpool's famous Champions League win in Istanbul attracted thousands, with Liverpool city centre packed with fans desperate for the glimpse of their beloved Reds.
If all goes well on June 1, even bigger crowds are expected along the route - but where will you be able to get a view of the bus and the team?
We've studied the route, which starts at 4pm, and this is where we'd suggest going:
- Standing close to Childwall Fiveways roundabout will mean you catch the start of the parade
- Queens Drive by the Rocket flyover
- The Jolly Miller pub in West Derby
- West Derby Road just after Sheil Road, opposite the Belmont Pub
- On the walkover on Hunter Street/Byron Street close to Liverpool John Moores University campus
- The garden of St Nick's Church, because you'll get a slightly higher view than just standing on The Strand
Of course, our photographers will be on stand by to bring you all the latest pictures of the crowds and the bus.
image_captions: Homecoming parade route 2019 if Liverpool Football Club win the Premier League and the UEFA Champions League Final
The Jolly Miller Pub, West Derby, Liverpool
